rulings.Case Title: DEBORAH HIGER VS. DIGNITY HEALTH, A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Non-Party Shasta View Nursing Center'S Compliance With Subpoena; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery/Law and Motion calendar for Thursday, Apr-03-2025, Line 6. 2-DEFENDANTS DR. DAVID HOLST, M.D., DR. RONALD K. LAGRO, M.D., and DIGNITIY HEALTH'S Motion To Compel Non-Party Shasta View Nursing Center'S Compliance With Subpoena. Defendants Dignity Health, Dr. David Holst, and Dr. Ronald K. Lagro move to compel non-party Shasta View Nursing Center (Shasta View) to comply with Defendants subpoena of records. No opposition was filed. The court denies the motion. Defendants served a subpoena on Shasta View seeking the personnel file for plaintiff Dr. Deborah Higer, who testified to her employment there. (Baca Dec. Exs. B, C.) Shasta View eventually submitted a declaration under penalty of perjury stating that it has no payroll or employment records for Higer. (Ex. E.) Defendants believe Higers discovery responses demonstrate that that cannot be correct. But the response is full and complete in itself, and sworn under penalty of perjury. The court agrees that it is a puzzling response (either May was untruthful, Higer was untruthful, Shasta View didnt keep employment or payment records, Shasta View destroyed its employment records, or similar farfetched possibilities), and Defendants may well have justification to seek, for instance, deposition testimony from the declarant or a Shasta View PMQ to explain the puzzling response. But the discovery statutes do not provide a basis for the court to test the truth of complete and nonevasive responses. The court does not award sanctions. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the courts website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under Online Services, navigate to Tentative Rulings, and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The text of the email shall include the name and contact information, including email address, of the attorney or party who will appear at the hearing. Counsel for the moving party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must email it to contestdept301tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. The court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law & Motion or Discovery Department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSRs transcript may be used in California courts. rulings.Case Title: SHELLEY MARDER ET AL VS. RANDIE BENCANNAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Additional Physical Examination Of Plaintiff Shelley Marder
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery/Law and Motion calendar for Thursday, Apr-03-2025, Line 8. DEFENDANT RANDIE BENCANNAN'S Motion To Compel Additional Physical Examination Of Plaintiff Shelley Marder. Defendants motion to compel an additional physical examination of plaintiff is denied without prejudice. A defendant is entitled to one physical examination of a plaintiff seeking personal injury damages. (CCP 2032.220(a).) To obtain a second examination, defendant must obtain leave of court based on good cause. (CCP 2032.310(a); CCP 2032.320(a); Vinson v. Superior Court (1987) 43 Cal.3d 833, 840-41.) In addition, a motion for a medical examination over objection must include a separate statement. (CRC 3.1345(a)(6).) Here, as defendant admits in the reply, defendant did not comply with the separate statement requirement. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) rulings.Case Title: JAVIER E. MARTINEZ VS. GENERAL MOTORS LLC, A DELAWARE LIMITED LIABILITY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel The Deposition Of Defendant'S Person Most Knowledgeable And Production Of Documents
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery/Law and Motion calendar for Thursday, Apr-03-2025, Line 9. PLAINTIFF JAVIER MARTINEZ', AN INDIVIDUAL Motion To Compel The Deposition Of Defendant'S Person Most Knowledgeable And Production Of Documents. Plaintiff Javier E. Martinezs unopposed motion to compel the deposition of defendant General Motors LLCs person most qualified and production of documents is granted. The parties are ordered to meet and confer and set a mutually agreeable date for the deposition within one week of entry of this order. The deposition must occur within 30 days of entry of this order unless the parties mutually agree otherwise. If the parties are unable to determine a mutually agreeable date, Martinez may set the deposition unilaterally to occur within 30 days of entry of this order. General Motors shall produce documents responsive to the deposition notices requests for production. CCP 2025.450(g)(1) provides: If a motion under subdivision (a) is granted, the court shall impose a monetary sanction under Chapter 7 (commencing with Section 2023.010) in favor of the party who noticed the deposition and against the deponent or the party with whom the deponent is affiliated, unless the court finds that the one subject to the sanction acted with substantial justification or that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust. The court does not find substantial justification and orders General Motors to pay $4720 in sanctions to Martinez within 30 days of entry of this order. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. rulings.Case Title: JASMINE GROTEGEER VS. SAN FRANCISCO CITY AND COUNTY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Attorneys Fees
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Discovery/Law and Motion calendar for Thursday, Apr-03-2025, Line 4. DEFENDANT ELISA MOLLICK'S Motion For Attorneys Fees. Hearing required. The court is inclined to grant this unopposed request but requires clarification about whether Andrea Goodman, whose anti-SLAPP motion was made in the same moving papers as moving party Elisa Mollick, will also be seeking fees, and if so, whether the fees set out in the moving papers are exclusively attributable to work done for Mollick. The courts tentative appears below. Moving party Elisa Mollick succeeded on a special motion to strike Plaintiff Jasmine Grotegeers claims against her pursuant to CCP Sec. 425.16. She now seeks fees in the amount of $15,360. For the reasons stated herein the court grants this unopposed motion. An award of fees and costs is mandatory to a prevailing defendant on an anti-SLAPP motion. (CCP 425.16 (c)(1).) [A] defendant who brings a successful special motion to strike is entitled only to reasonable attorney fees, and not necessarily to the entire amount requested. (Cabral v. Martins (2009) 177 Cal.App.4th 471, 491.) To determine a reasonable amount, [t]he amount of an attorney fee award under the anti-SLAPP statute is computed by the trial court in accordance with the familiar lodestar method. Under that method, the court tabulates the attorney fee touchstone, or lodestar, by multiplying the number of hours reasonably expended by the reasonable hourly rate prevailing in the community for similar work. (Id. [quotation marks and citations omitted].) After review of the anti-SLAPP moving papers and the breakdown of fees set out in Exhibit E of the Graft Declaration, the court finds that the number of hours expended was reasonable. The court also finds that the hourly rates are reasonable based on paragraphs 12-17 of the Graft Declaration. Mollick is entitled to fees on feesthat is, fees she incurred in bringing this fee motion. (See Ketchum v. Moses (2001) 24 Cal.4th 1122, 1141.) Because the court finds the fee request reasonable using the lodestar method, it awards $15,360 to Mollick payable by Grotegeer. For the 9:00 a.m. calendar, all attorneys and parties may appear in Department 301 remotely or in person. Remote hearings will be conducted by videoconference using Zoom. (Dept. 301 Zoom ID 161 502 4290; Passcode 700956.) To appear remotely at the hearing, go to the courts website at sfsuperiorcourt.org under Online Services, navigate to Tentative Rulings, and click on the appropriate link, or dial the corresponding phone number. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept301tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests.
